servers.name: kubernetes
servers.description: Read-only Kubernetes access (resources, pods, logs, Helm release listing). --read-only exposes only readOnlyHint tools at the source; the allowlist pins the exact set. Cerbos blocks reading Secrets. Runs on the 'vicegerent' docker network to reach the API, which widens the host trust boundary to that network (see host/mcp/README). configuration_view is excluded despite being read-only: it returns the full kubeconfig with client cert/key in plaintext (a usable cluster credential); configuration_contexts_list covers context discovery without key material.

params.name: kubeconfig
params.prompt: Kubeconfig path for the target cluster (blank = auto-generate from the kind 'vicegerent' cluster)

servers.name: gitlab
servers.description: GitLab MCP scoped to issues (read+write) and the merge-request lifecycle short of merging or approving (create/update/review/comment/labels), plus read-only pipeline status/logs. No generic git file/branch-write tools and no repo/project allowlist: the bot has direct SSH to gitlab.hahomelabs.com, so routine git goes through git itself and MCP is only for MR-object actions git can't do. approve/unapprove and merge are excluded (HAH-64) so the bot can't rubber-stamp or merge any MR. execute_graphql is excluded (arbitrary API access would bypass every other exclusion).

servers.name: github
servers.description: GitHub MCP scoped to PRs only — no issue tools (this operator doesn't use GitHub issues at work) and no generic git file/branch-write tools, since the bot has direct SSH to github.com and does routine git with git itself. Surface: get_me and the PR lifecycle short of merging or leaving any review/comment text. All comment-reply and review-write tools (add_reply_to_pull_request_comment, add_comment_to_pending_review, pull_request_review_write) are excluded on operator instruction — the bot leaves NO comment/review text on PRs, and there's no argument-level signal to distinguish acceptable from not, so the capability is removed rather than partially enforced. Cerbos (defs/resource_github.yaml) hard-enforces the target owner/repo and branch regardless of token scope, and forces every create/update_pull_request to draft:true.

servers.name: tavily
servers.description: Tavily web search and content extraction. No write capability exists (search/extract/crawl/map/research against arbitrary public URLs, nothing that touches anything this platform owns), so the tools list below is the full live set pinned explicitly rather than a restriction.

servers.name: firecrawl
servers.description: Firecrawl web scraping, crawling, and extraction. No write capability exists (every tool reads/scrapes/monitors an arbitrary public URL you supply; nothing touches anything this platform owns), so the tools list below is the full live set pinned explicitly rather than a restriction.

servers.name: notion
servers.description: Notion workspace (official remote MCP; OAuth, browser consent on first start). Read broad (search/fetch/comments/teams/users); write narrow (create-pages/update-page/create-comment; no database/move/duplicate). Cerbos (defs/resource_notion.yaml) pins create-pages to the Scratchpad folder, and gates update-page/create-comment via a live shim ancestry lookup against ${notionAllowedParentPageIds} (Scratchpad always implicit), additionally denying replace_content/allow_deleting_content on pages that pass. A read-broad/write-narrow integration split was infeasible — the org blocks internal-integration tokens.

servers.name: linear
servers.description: Linear issues and projects (OAuth; browser consent on first start). Scoped to the platform's original functional set — issues/comments/projects/labels/statuses/teams/users/docs, read+write, no deletes — out of Linear's full 55-tool surface. save_issue/save_comment/save_project are Linear's merged create+update tools (an `id` arg picks which). Cerbos (defs/resource_linear.yaml) confines save_issue calls that supply a team to DEVOPS. Newer feature categories and destructive deletes are excluded pending a follow-up decision.

servers.name: jira
servers.description: Jira (sooperset/mcp-atlassian container), confined to the CHANGE project. Jira-only (no Confluence creds). JIRA_PROJECTS_FILTER=CHANGE scopes search server-side; cross-project enumeration tools are dropped. Read+write (issues, JQL, transitions, comments), no deletes. Cerbos (defs/resource_jira.yaml) hard-enforces the CHANGE boundary by project_key or issue/epic-key prefix. NB the official remote (io.github.stacklok/atlassian-remote) does NOT work through our vMCP — it advertises the resources capability but returns method-not-found for resources/list, so the health probe drops it from aggregation.

servers.name: grafana
servers.description: Grafana observability, read-only (search/datasource/dashboard/prometheus/asserts/annotations/rendering reads). --disable-write is the fail-closed write kill-switch. --disable-proxied turns off Tempo-datasource MCP discovery (unused): without it, being off-VPN makes every session across ALL vMCP backends hang on a live call to Grafana, not just this one. Cerbos additionally blocks reads of OpenSearch datasources.

servers.name: grafana_gov
servers.description: Grafana observability (gov-cloud instance) -- otherwise identical to 'grafana' (see that entry for the tool/write-lockdown and --disable-proxied rationale). Separate secrets (grafana_gov_url/grafana_gov_service_account_token) so it points at its own instance.

servers.name: alertmanager
servers.description: Prometheus Alertmanager (alerts, silences, alert groups, status). Full read+write, including createSilence/deleteSilence — the tools list below is the full live 12-tool set pinned explicitly rather than a restriction.

servers.name: alertmanager_gov
servers.description: Prometheus Alertmanager (gov-cloud instance) -- otherwise identical to 'alertmanager' (see that entry for the tool-set rationale). The url param is secret-backed and auto-namespaced (alertmanager_gov_url), so it can't collide with alertmanager's.

servers.name: pagerduty
servers.description: PagerDuty, read-only incident/schedule/on-call/service/team/user lookups plus ack/resolve/note on existing incidents (manage_incidents, add_note_to_incident). create_incident, add_responders, and start_incident_workflow are NOT exposed — triage only, no incident creation or responder escalation from the agent. Cerbos (defs/resource_pagerduty.yaml) scopes manage_incidents to ack/resolve-only field changes.

servers.name: pagerduty_gov
servers.description: PagerDuty (gov-cloud instance) -- otherwise identical to 'pagerduty' (see that entry for the tool/scoping rationale). Separate secret (pagerduty_gov_user_api_key) so it authenticates against its own account.

servers.name: elastic
servers.description: Elastic Agent Builder (Kibana) via ToolHive's native `remote` streamable-http transport, pointed at /api/agent_builder/mcp and authed by a static `Authorization: ApiKey <key>` header injected from the thv secret store (--remote-forward-headers-secret, api_key's apply:remote_header) so the credential never enters argv. Uses the native remote transport, NOT the mcp-remote npx bridge: mcp-remote's mandatory MCP-SDK OAuth discovery GETs /.well-known/oauth-* which this Kibana 302-redirects to an HTML login page, crashing its JSON parser; ToolHive's transport only runs discovery under --remote-auth (never passed here), so plain header auth connects directly. kibana_url is both the run positional (registry_from_param) and the egress allow_host; the api_key secret is injected verbatim as the Authorization header, so it must carry the 'ApiKey ' scheme prefix -- `configure` prepends that automatically (the param's value_prefix), so the operator pastes just the bare key. Kibana serves on 443. Read/search/analysis tools only — the 3 write tools (platform_core_create_visualization, security_create_detection_rule, platform_core_resume_workflow_execution) are excluded, so no Cerbos guardrail is needed yet.

params.name: kibana_url
params.prompt: Elastic Kibana Agent Builder MCP URL, e.g. https://<your-kibana-host>/api/agent_builder/mcp (no port suffix -- Kibana serves this on the HTTPS default 443)

params.name: api_key
params.prompt: Elastic API key -- paste just the encoded key from Kibana Stack Management > Security > API keys (read-only Agent Builder role). The 'ApiKey ' Authorization scheme prefix is added automatically.

servers.name: aws
servers.description: AWS access via awslabs aws-api-mcp-server, read-only and multi-profile. Runs a thin non-blocking patch of the upstream image (images/aws-api-mcp-server): the upstream server executes the AWS call synchronously on its asyncio event loop, so a long call (e.g. list-secrets paginating a whole account) blocks its MCP protocol and freezes the vMCP's shared-deadline aggregation for every backend; the patch offloads that work to a worker thread via asyncio.to_thread. READ_OPERATIONS_ONLY=true restricts call_aws to read operations (allowlist-enforced at the source; pair each profile with a ReadOnlyAccess-scoped IAM role -- the README is explicit that IAM is the primary control). The host ~/.aws is mounted READ-ONLY at /app/.aws (apply:aws_config, HOME pinned to /app so the SSO token cache resolves; the server's own writable state dir is overlaid separately), exposing every installed profile; the agent selects one per call with `aws ... --profile <name>` (AWS_API_MCP_PROFILE_NAME left unset, so there's no default). SSO tokens are refreshed host-side with `aws sso login` -- the read-only mount means the sandbox never writes the operator's AWS creds, matching how every other backend's credentials are maintained outside the sandbox. Exposes call_aws (executes an AWS CLI command string, or a batch list of up to 20) and suggest_aws_commands (natural-language -> suggested CLI; note it sends the query to an AWS-hosted endpoint under api.aws); get_execution_plan stays off (EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_SCRIPTS unset). Egress is allowlisted to .amazonaws.com and .api.aws (leading-dot suffix -- ToolHive's squid does dstdomain matching, so this one suffix covers every <service>.<region>.amazonaws.com across all regions incl. gov, the SSO oidc/portal endpoints, and the read-only-classification endpoint; .api.aws covers newer endpoints + the suggest_aws_commands endpoint api-mcp.global.api.aws). Verified live via the egress proxy's access log: sts/ec2/etc. across regions tunnel through, while aws.amazon.com and non-AWS hosts are denied. The mcp-cerbos-shim guardrail (resource_aws.yaml) denies call_aws commands that mint credentials/tokens/access (eks get-token, sts assume-role, ecr/rds/redshift auth tokens, kms decrypt, private-key/cert exports, console-credential creation, ...) or read secret values (Secrets Manager get/batch-get-secret-value, SSM get-parameter*) -- verified live that READ_OPERATIONS_ONLY does NOT block these (it even force-allows some minters, e.g. eks get-token returns a real Kubernetes bearer token). Scoping each profile's IAM permission set is the airtight complement.

params.name: aws_config_dir
params.prompt: Path to your AWS config dir (blank = ~/.aws). Mounted read-only; refresh SSO tokens host-side with `aws sso login`.

servers.name: aws_profiles
servers.description: Hidden companion to the `aws` backend (companion_of: aws) -- enabled/disabled with it as one unit, never shown or configured on its own, so a developer doesn't need to know it exists. One tool, `list`, returning the AWS profile names from the mounted read-only ~/.aws/config so the agent can discover which --profile values call_aws accepts (aws-api-mcp-server can't enumerate profiles -- its validator rejects `aws configure`). Read-only, network.none (makes no outbound calls -- reads the local config and serves stdio through ToolHive's bridge), returns profile NAMES only, never credentials. Reuses the same apply:aws_config read-only ~/.aws mount as the aws backend (its aws_config_dir param value is inherited from the parent). Tiny custom image (images/aws-profiles-mcp). Workload named `aws_profiles` with tool `list` so the vMCP's {workload}_ prefix surfaces it as `aws_profiles_list`, sitting naturally in the aws_ tool family next to aws_call_aws / aws_suggest_aws_commands (two workloads can't share the exact `aws` prefix -- names are unique per group -- so this is the closest natural fit).

params.name: aws_config_dir
params.prompt: Path to your AWS config dir (blank = ~/.aws). Mounted read-only.
